summary: Support BSON Timestamp
Breaking changes
description:
The ruby driver is constantly suffering breaking changes in its API. Last one I found is the disappearance of Connection#host. Wether or not the method should have been discarded is beyond the scope of this post. But a method should never disappear between a "1.1.2" and "1.1.5" version. A minor version number update should not break client code. Moreover, this particular change was not listed in the changelog.

This is not the first time this library have suffered for such changes, we all remember the ObjectID to ObjectId change last year.

We came to a point where we have more issues when we bump the ruby driver than when we bump the server itself. This does not make any sense at all. It's a shame to impair whatever credibility everybody works so hard to give to mongodb by failure to comply to simple good practices in software engineering on such a simple matter as a mere client library.

Sorry for this rant. The bug is in the process here. I would be glad to provide a patch, but I can't.

summary: Enforce Semantic Versioning
description:
1. Enforce semantic versioning as described here: http://semver.org/

2. Document in Ruby driver docs for reference.

summary: Socket timeouts
description: Socket timeouts are not straightforward in Ruby. There are lot of bad ways of doing it. Investigate other libraries and come up with a good solution.

summary: Ruby driver gem does not include JAR's required when running with JRuby
description:
I'm running my ruby/rails app using JRuby and I've recently upgraded MongoDB from 1.6 to 1.8 and subsequently updated bson to 1.2.4 and the mongo ruby driver to 1.2.4. However, my tests are now failing. The error I'm getting is from the bson gem, specifically bson.rb (line 59) where it's trying to load the "mongo-2.4.jar".

The bson-1.2.4 gem appears to be looking for JAR's within bson-1.2.4/ext/java/jar but they do not exist (as they did with bson-1.1.2). I looked at the mongo-ruby-driver source (https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver) and see it now includes the JAR's, however when I download/install the mongo-ruby-driver 1.2.4 it does not include the "ext" directory. Meaning, /home/craig/.gems/gems/mongo-1.2.4 does not contain the "ext" directory.

I believe the mongo-ruby-driver gem needs to be fixed to include the JAR's and the bson gem needs to be fixed to look for the JARs contained within the mongo-ruby-driver gem (once it's been fixed).

summary: ReplSetConnection only connects if the first argument is the primary
description:
The order of the arguments to Mongo::ReplSetConnection#new seems to affect whether it connects: (mongo02 is the primary)

  irb(main):001:0> Mongo::ReplSetConnection.new(['mongo01', 27017], ['mongo02', 27017])
  Mongo::ConnectionFailure: Failed to connect to primary node.
  	from gems/mongo-1.3.0/lib/../lib/mongo/repl_set_connection.rb:128:in `connect'
  	from gems/mongo-1.3.0/lib/../lib/mongo/connection.rb:632:in `setup'
  	from gems/mongo-1.3.0/lib/../lib/mongo/repl_set_connection.rb:93:in `initialize'
  	from (irb):1:in `new'
  	from (irb):1
  irb(main):002:0> Mongo::ReplSetConnection.new(['mongo02', 27017], ['mongo01', 27017])
  => #<Mongo::ReplSetConnection:0x117f0d30 @nodes_tried=[["mongo02", 27017], ["mongo02", 27017], ["mongo01", 27017], ["db01", 27017]], @safe=false, @pool_size=1, @read_secondary=false, @auths=[], @secondaries=[], @primary=["mongo02", 27017], @timeout=5.0, @id_lock=#<Mutex:0x117f0bc8>, @read_pool=nil, @primary_pool=#<Mongo::Pool:0x117ef2f0 @checked_out=[], @socket_ops={#<TCPSocket:0x117ee008>=>[]}, @timeout=5.0, @size=1, @pids={#<TCPSocket:0x117ee008>=>25920}, @port=27017, @queue=#<ConditionVariable:0x117ef250>, @sockets=[#<TCPSocket:0x117ee008>], @host="mongo02", @connection=#<Mongo::ReplSetConnection:0x117f0d30 ...>, @connection_mutex=#<Mutex:0x117ef278>>, @replica_set=nil, @logger=nil, @queue=#<ConditionVariable:0x117f0b28>, @secondary_pools=[], @nodes=[["mongo02", 27017], ["mongo01", 27017], ["db01", 27017]], @nodes_to_try=[], @op_timeout=nil, @safe_mutexes={#<TCPSocket:0x117f0998>=>#<Mutex:0x117eff70>, #<TCPSocket:0x117ee008>=>#<Mutex:0x117edea0>}, @connection_mutex=#<Mutex:0x117f0ba0>, @arbiters=[]>
  irb(main):003:0> 


Logs from the slave when it is the first argument:

Tue Apr 12 17:23:15 [initandlisten] connection accepted from XX.XX.XX.XX:58122 #49
Tue Apr 12 17:23:15 [conn49] end connection XX.XX.XX.XX:58122

The primary shows no connection attempts at all.

summary: Mongo::ConnectionFailure: Failed to connect to primary node when primary node is not first to try
description:
The test replicaset setup is (on my Mac): 

10.18.101.86:10001 (secondary)
10.18.101.86:10002 (primary)
10.18.101.86:10003 (arbiter)

When I did: (from a ubuntu vm)
  
  Mongo::ReplSetConnection.new(["10.18.101.86", 10001], ["10.18.101.86", 10002], ["10.18.101.86", 10003])
or 
  Mongo::ReplSetConnection.new(["10.18.101.86", 10003], ["10.18.101.86", 10002], ["10.18.101.86", 10001])

I got exception: 

Mongo::ConnectionFailure: Failed to connect to primary node.
	from ./lib/../lib/../lib/mongo/repl_set_connection.rb:128:in `connect'
	from ./lib/../lib/../lib/mongo/connection.rb:632:in `setup'
	from ./lib/../lib/../lib/mongo/repl_set_connection.rb:93:in `initialize'
	from (irb):3:in `new'
	from (irb):3

But if I change the order of nodes, and place the primary node (10.18.101.86:10002 ) as the first in the nodes list I can successfully create ReplSetConnection: 

ree-1.8.7-2010.02 > Mongo::ReplSetConnection.new(["10.18.101.86", 10002], ["10.18.101.86", 10001], ["10.18.101.86", 10003])
 => #<Mongo::ReplSetConnection:0x320be28 @connection_mutex=#<Mutex:0x320bc48>, @read_secondary=false, @secondaries=[], @primary_pool=#<Mongo::Pool:0x320a118 @pids={#<TCPSocket:0x3208610>=>12332}, @connection_mutex=#<Mutex:0x320a0a0>, @host="10.18.101.86", @sockets=[#<TCPSocket:0x3208610>], @timeout=5.0, @connection=#<Mongo::ReplSetConnection:0x320be28 ...>, @socket_ops={#<TCPSocket:0x3208610>=>[]}, @size=1, @checked_out=[], @queue=#<ConditionVariable:0x320a078>, @port=10002>, @op_timeout=nil, @auths=[], @safe=false, @timeout=5.0, @read_pool=nil, @primary=["10.18.101.86", 10002], @replica_set=nil, @nodes_to_try=[], @safe_mutexes={#<TCPSocket:0x3208610>=>#<Mutex:0x32084a8>, #<TCPSocket:0x320ba40>=>#<Mutex:0x320af00>}, @pool_size=1, @nodes=[["localhost", 10002], ["localhost", 10001], ["localhost", 10003]], @secondary_pools=[], @nodes_tried=[["10.18.101.86", 10002], ["localhost", 10002], ["localhost", 10001], ["localhost", 10003]], @logger=nil, @queue=#<ConditionVariable:0x320bbd0>, @id_lock=#<Mutex:0x320bc70>, @arbiters=[]> 

Another thing is that, if I connect the replicaset on the host machine (mac) where replicaset is on, the order doesn't matter.

summary: BSON::OrderedHash#reject! leaves keys with nil values
Usage of GridIO#gets runs into an infinite loop when reaching the end of a file
description:
GridIO#gets doesn't return nil when reaching the end of a file when used with a single or multi-character separator.  This causes common usage of the method run infinitely, while waiting for a nil that will never come.

Please see these pull requests:

https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/pull/40
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/pull/41

summary: Mongo::DB should support symbols for collection names in all use cases
description:
Methods in Mongo::DB that check collection_names.include?(name) don't find a collection when the collection name is passed as a symbol. This doesn't match the behavior when the collection name passed in is used to instantiate a Mongo::Collection instance which does support  symbols for collection names.

My team ran into an issue where we ran Mongo::DB#drop_dollection(:collection_name) and didn't catch that it was a no-op since it returned true. The drop_collection method is coded to return true quickly if it doesn't find the name, and it does that comparison against an array of strings.

Mongo::DB methods affected: create_collection (in strict mode), collection (in strict mode), and drop_collection.

summary: Support regexp option "s" (/m in Ruby)
Cursor#close hits the wrong machine with :read_secondary
Cursor max scan
Ensure that JRuby deserialized binary type 0 as type 0 (not as type 2)
collection.map_reduce method fails when :raw option is specified
description:
The commit (8887402ca81f3d4c63c10126577861711475e80c) changed the way options are treated, but that resulted in passing :raw parameter to the server which results in a failure with the error:

Database command 'mapreduce' failed: {"ok"=>0.0, "errmsg"=>"unknown m/r field for sharding: raw"} (Mongo::OperationFailure)
/usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9.1/gems/mongo-1.3.1/lib/mongo/db.rb:506:in `command'
/usr/local/lib/ruby/gems/1.9.1/gems/mongo-1.3.1/lib/mongo/collection.rb:576:in `map_reduce'

I've sent a github pull request with the fix.

summary: BSON::OrderedHash can be corrupted
description:
BSON::OrderedHash can be corrupted

steps to reproduce:
execute the following lines:

  a = BSON::OrderedHash.new
  a[:b] = :c
  a.replace(:c => :d)

results

a is corrupted.  For instance it inspects as:
   {"b"=>nil}
instead of:
   {:c => :d}

the method 'replace' on Hash is not overridden !!!!!!! 

implementation suggestion:

  def replace(other_hash)
    @ordered_keys.replace(other_hash.keys)
    super
  end

summary: Reads should not be sent to recovering nodes
description: Only the primary and secondaries.

summary: Neither use nor recommend the use of SystemTimer.
description: This is necessary because SystemTimer isn't thread-safe.

summary: Generic add_option for cursor
Connection Timeout Option for the Ruby Driver
description:
Right now if the Ruby driver tries to set up a connection to a host that is unreachable or nonexistent, the connection attempt appears to last ~30 seconds before timing out.

If possible, it would be nice for the MongoDB Ruby driver to have a configurable connect timeout option. Specifically, we'd like to be able to set a timeout on setting up a connection to a host — not a timeout on socket reads (which is configurable using the :op_timeout option).

summary: BSON::OrderedHash#inspect is confusing
description:
Affecting values to a BSON ordered hash is working OK with string or symbol values, but the inspect is quite confusing (example from an IRB session) :

ree-1.8.7-2011.03 :001 > bson_hash = BSON::OrderedHash.new
 => {} 
ree-1.8.7-2011.03 :002 > bson_hash['hello'] = 'bonjour'
 => "bonjour" 
ree-1.8.7-2011.03 :003 > bson_hash[:hello] = 'salut'
 => "salut" 
ree-1.8.7-2011.03 :004 > bson_hash
 => {"hello"=>"bonjour", "hello"=>"salut"}


I don't really know what would be the best way to do this, but as it is I find it confusing.

summary: cursor.isAlive?
Add cursor.addOption/removeOption(int)

summary: Add a capped method to the Collection object
description: Add a capped method to the Collection object that returns a boolean value indicating whether the collection is capped or not.  This can be obtained using the stats method and by finding the "capped" key value in the hash.

summary: Timestamp typo
Fix potential infinite loop
description:
Same bug as this one:
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/pull/43

summary: Logging issue in Collection#find_one
description:
Then calling find_one, it merge :limit => -1 option to selector. So limit options don't appear in log.

This confusing, then during debugging session you get some thing like this in log:
MONGODB my_test['managers'].find({})

The first idea is why I fetch all records??? And only in profiler you can see, that actually only one record was fetched.

summary: Test suite included in Bson gem doesn't work
description:
The test suite included in Bson gem is incomplete and cannot work. Please either 

a) Fix the test suite to be selfcontained
b) Do not include the test suite at all

I would prefer the A, which would allow me to run quick test during build of Bson gem for Fedora, but any solution of these two is better then the current situation IMO.

summary: Update for changes to gotBinaryArray
description:
I just updated the interface and impl. so that we can remove the
gotBinaryArray callback (without the type); for now it will be
deprecated and then removed soon.

https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-java-driver/blob/master/src/main/org/bson/BSONCallback.java#L46
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-java-driver/blob/master/src/main/org/bson/BasicBSONCallback.java#L133

This means you should be able to cleanup the code in ruby post
java-2.6.1; I think this is what you wanted and it should make the
decoding cleaner and more clear in general. It will be a breaking
change for 2.7, fyi.

summary: BSON can't serialize instances of Set
description:
Just tried to make a query such as:

  { '_id' => {'$in' => my_set }

where `my_set` is an instance of ruby's Set class, part of stdlib. BSON complained that it can't serialize it.

Since a set behaves as an array as far as we're concerned here, I recommend improving BSON serialization to treat Sets as arrays.

summary: Use 'sync' for RW lock on RepSetConnection
Added a nice #inspect method for Node, PoolManager, ReplSetConnection, and Pool
Timestamp with bson_ext
description:
From a user:

Hello, I have the following problem with bson_ext:

I'm trying to poll oplog with tailable cursor. To skip already processed records in case when everything fails (cursor need to be re-queried), I have to persist last processed record's timestamp somewhere (obviously, in mongo:)). When bson_ext is not used, everything works fine. 

When I use bson_ext, starting from some value some strange things happens with the seconds of the timestamp I  save into database using native serializer. 
Here are sympthoms:
beginning from some value near 10^6-7 (which makes me think there is something about integer overflow) seconds field values begin to differ for native serialization and Ruby one. What is fancier, that on Linux (Debian) and Windows 
overflown 
values for bson_ext are different.

Can someone tell me, what can be done about that and can something be done at all:)? For now speed of Ruby serialization is sufficient, but it would be nice to have maximum performance.

I can provide more information and tests code if required.

summary: Add cascading :read API
description: The Ruby driver limits the use of slave_ok just to the connection level but it should be supported (both true and false values) in a cascading fashion on Connection, DB, db operations, Collection, collection operations, and Cursor. Some of the other drivers already support much of this.
